topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music was a vibrant, insistent thrum against my temples. Every laugh, every clinking glass, seemed to vibrate right through my bones, making the edges of my vision soften. I leaned against a marble pillar, trying to anchor myself to the solid coolness of the stone, but my eyelids felt weighted with lead. Then, across the crowded ballroom, a familiar silhouette caught my eye. It was him. Liam. He was laughing, his head tilted back, and the way the light caught the silver in his hair was exactly the same as I remembered. A slow, heavy warmth began to spread through my chest, making the effort of staying upright feel monumental. I took a slow, deliberate sip of my lukewarm champagne, the liquid tasting flat, and found myself just watching him, the world around him blurring into soft focus.

[story 2]

I was supposed to be circulating, making introductions, but the energy in the room was just too much. The sheer volume of happy noise was pressing in, making my thoughts slow to a crawl. My eyes kept drifting toward the entrance, and there he was—my former partner, standing near the cake table, looking impossibly polished. A strange, languid pull tugged at my limbs, a desire to just sink into the nearest velvet armchair and let the noise fade. I managed a strained smile for a passing acquaintance, but it felt brittle. When our gazes briefly locked across the expanse of the room, a profound sense of inertia washed over me. I blinked slowly, trying to sharpen my focus, but the edges of the scene began to soften, pulling me toward a deep, heavy quietude.

[story 3]

The air in the reception hall was thick with perfume and celebration, and I found myself leaning heavily against a potted palm, needing a moment of stillness. My movements felt sluggish, like wading through thick honey. I was trying to keep up with the conversation, nodding vaguely at anecdotes about the couple, but my attention kept snagging on a familiar figure near the dance floor. It was Sarah’s wedding, and there, bathed in the warm glow of string lights, was Alex. Seeing him brought a sudden, unexpected slowing down to my internal rhythm. A profound sense of relaxation, almost a surrender, settled over me. I let my head drop slightly, the effort of maintaining alertness draining away, and for a suspended moment, I simply allowed myself to drift.
